title: Aerobic Composting — Temperature, Moisture, and Gas Indicators
content: Aerobic composting passes through four temperature phases: mesophilic (10–40 °C, weeks 1–2), thermophilic (40–70 °C, weeks 2–4), cooling (40–50 °C), and maturation (ambient). Optimal moisture: 40–60 % by weight; CO₂ production peaks during thermophilic phase; CH₄ (methane) indicates anaerobic zones.
extended_content: Key monitoring parameters: (1) Temperature — thermophilic phase (55–65 °C for 3 days) kills pathogens per EPA Part 503. (2) Moisture — below 40 % slows microbial activity; above 65 % causes anaerobic pockets and odour (NH₃, H₂S). (3) O₂/CO₂ ratio — healthy aerobic composting: O₂ > 10 %, CO₂ < 10 %. (4) C:N ratio — optimal 25:1 to 30:1 by dry mass. Sensor implementation: DS18B20 probes at multiple depths for temperature mapping; capacitive soil moisture sensors adapted for compost moisture; NDIR CO₂ sensors (SCD41) for headspace CO₂; MQ-4 or MQ-2 gas sensors for methane detection. Turning frequency: every 3–5 days during thermophilic phase to maintain aerobic conditions. Maturity indicators: temperature returns to ambient, CO₂ drops to < 2 %, stable dark colour.
source: Haug, The Practical Handbook of Compost Engineering, CRC Press, 1993; EPA 40 CFR Part 503
